summary:
Nelson Mandela was released from Victor Verster Prison on February 11, 1990, after 27 years of imprisonment, becoming an immediate symbol of the coming end of [[event:2684|apartheid]] and South Africa's transition to democracy. Mandela had been imprisoned since 1964, when he was convicted of sabotage and conspiracy to overthrow the government following the Rivonia Trial, and sentenced to life imprisonment on Robben Island. His release was announced by South African President F.W. de Klerk, who simultaneously unbanned the African National Congress and other anti-apartheid organizations and suspended executions.

Mandela's imprisonment had made him the most famous political prisoner in the world. The international campaign for his release — "Free Nelson Mandela" — became one of the most successful solidarity movements in history, with the 1984 Artists United Against Apartheid recording of the song helping bring his cause to global popular culture. The United Nations had called for his release for decades. His continued imprisonment was a primary driver of the international sanctions and cultural boycotts that helped make apartheid economically and politically untenable.

Mandela walked out of prison holding the hand of his then-wife Winnie Mandela, raised his fist in the ANC's trademark salute, and addressed a crowd of thousands in Cape Town. He explicitly declined to take a path of vengeance, calling for negotiations that would lead to a democratic South Africa. His first appearance signaled his continued authority within the ANC and his commitment to a negotiated, non-violent transition to majority rule.

In the four years following his release, Mandela led the ANC's negotiations with the de Klerk government, ultimately producing the agreement to hold the multiracial [[event:2772|elections of 1994]] and the interim constitution. He and de Klerk jointly received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993. Mandela won the presidency in South Africa's first fully democratic election in April 1994, serving as president until 1999 and overseeing the [[event:3490|Truth and Reconciliation Commission]] process.

Mandela's release and subsequent presidency represent one of history's most remarkable transformations — from long-imprisoned political prisoner to head of state, achieved without the civil war that many had feared. His example of reconciliation over retribution, and his ability to embody simultaneously both the struggle against racial oppression and the path toward multiracial coexistence, made him one of the most admired figures of the twentieth century.
